data.title: Weibo Corporation: The Pluses Outnumber The Minuses
data.description:
Weibo Corporation (NASDAQ:WB), a social media platform in China, put the final touches on FY2024 with the release of its Form-20F on April 15. The recently concluded fiscal was a year in which, among other things, the top and the bottom line ended flat to slightly down, but all this was overshadowed by the recent turmoil in the stock market, which did not leave WB unscathed. However, while a further decline in the stock cannot be ruled out in the current environment, long WB still looks like the way to go. Why will be covered next.

Prior concerns turn into reality

A previous article from early February raised the issue of possible U.S. tariffs on China and what it could mean for a stock like WB, which some may have seen as a stock that is safe from tariffs because it does not export goods to the U.S and is therefore not subject to U.S. tariffs. However, the article noted how WB could be affected by a tariff dispute between the U.S. and China in not a direct way, but in an indirect way, which could cause problems for the stock. In the end, while the article singled out trade disputes as a wild card for WB, the article nonetheless rated WB a buy because its positives outweighed the negatives over the long run.

The chart above shows why the article was right to point out the potential impact of tariffs on a stock like WB because there certainly was an impact on WB with the stock collapsing in the month of March after it became clear the trade dispute centered on tariffs between the U.S. and China was about to reach a fever pitch. WB started the month of March with substantial YTD gains, but the collapse in the last four weeks or so saw WB go from $10.90 per ADS on March 17 to an intraday low of $7.10 on April 9, which means WB lost as much as 35% of its market cap in the past four weeks at one point.

Why the stock’s decline could be seen as an opportunity

WB has fallen, but that also means the stock goes for much less than it did not too long ago. The stock has moved higher in recent days after closing at $7.71 per ADS on April 15, along with the stock market as a whole thanks to a pause in tariffs, although the situation regarding China remains as tense as ever. Yet WB remains oversold with an RSI reading in the twenties, even with the recent bounce in the stock.

Furthermore, the charts suggest support is in place for WB. Notice how in the prior chart, the stock bottomed at $7.10 on April 9, which is within pennies of where WB bottomed on September 9, 2024 at $7.03. In fact, there are several examples of the stock halting a decline in or close to the $7-7.50 region with the earliest example coming in January 2024, as shown in the chart below for the past two years. The most recent bounce in the stock in the $7-7.50 region suggests support, which has held for quite some time, continues to stand its ground. This is a positive signal for the bulls.

There is support out there for WB, but that is not all. The case to be long WB got a boost with WB recently announcing that it will pay an annual dividend going forward. The latest dividend of $0.82 per ADS is equal to the one paid last year, but the difference is that last year’s dividend was a special dividend, unlike this year. There are no guarantees WB will continue to pay a dividend of $0.82 per ADS, which will cost WB about $200M, but if it does, such a dividend implies a yield of 10.6% with the ADS at $7.71. This compares very well to other stocks, tech stocks in particular, and is another plus for WB.

Why WB can be said to be undervalued according to one measure

In addition, valuations for WB are so low to the point it can be argued that WB is undervalued in some ways. In the most recent or FY2024 report, WB reported revenue of $1,754.68M, which resulted in GAAP earnings of $300.8M or $1.16 per ADS and non-GAAP earnings of $478.6M or $1.82 per ADS. In comparison, WB reported $1,759.84M, $1.43 and $1.88, respectively, in FY2023.

The bottom line was somewhat worse off YoY, but it’s worth mentioning that FY2024 GAAP earnings were negatively affected by an impairment charge to the tune of $91.9M. If not for this, GAAP EPADS could have been higher by another $0.35 per ADS. FY2024 GAAP income from operations was actually higher at $494.32M compared to $472.83M in FY2023. FY2024 operating margin was 28% in terms of GAAP and 34% in terms of non-GAAP. Overall, business was more or less stable, especially if exchange rate fluctuations and one-time charges are factored in.

These results give WB a P/E ratio of 6.6x in terms of GAAP and a P/E ratio of 4.2x in terms of non-GAAP with the ADS at $7.71. Such multiples compare very well against most stocks, especially those in the social media sphere. On the other hand, while WB continues to be a profitable company, there is no denying earnings are lower compared to the heights seen a few years ago. This is due in part to competition from other social media platforms in China, like WeChat or Douyin, the version of TikTok used in China, but it also reflects the slowing down of the Chinese economy.

Still, WB is undervalued according to at least one metric. WB has a market cap of about $1,888M with the ADS at $7.71 and the total number of ADS/ADRs at about 244.82M. In contrast, WB ended FY2024 with cash and cash equivalents of $1,890.63M, or $7.71 per ADS, which is by itself equal to what the ADS is priced at.

Total debt at WB stood at $1.86B, but this is offset by short-term investments totaling $459.85M and long-term investments totaling $1,389.20M. If everything is added up, then WB has a book value of $3,578.89M with total assets of $6,504.50M and total liabilities of $2,925.61M, which converts to a book value of $14.62 per ADS. WB is valued at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.53x and the ADS at $7.71. An argument can therefore be made that WB is undervalued based on the balance sheet.

What are the risks hanging over WB?

In the short term, tariffs are the most immediate risk to WB. Keep in mind WB depends heavily on advertising revenue from companies like Alibaba (BABA), but if the Chinese economy slows down due to less exports as a result of tariffs, then companies could try to cut back on advertising and that will affect WB.

WB has already been negatively affected by the slowing down of the Chinese economy, which used to grow significantly faster. A potential trade war between the U.S. and China could add to the pressure. At the same time, China does have options in the event the economy slows down too much. A major reason why Chinese stocks, including WB, have done better starting in late 2024 is because the Chinese government signaled it is open to monetary/fiscal stimulus. This could offset the impact of tariffs, in whole or in part.

However, in the long run, the risk WB is eclipsed by the competition is there. WB faces risks from competing social media platforms, which together with slower growth in China are arguably the two main factors that have caused earnings to take a dip in recent years, even if they remain at a relatively high level. Yet there is no doubt that growth could use improvement and something WB should work on.

WB is a smaller player when it comes to social media in China with MAUs of 590M and DAUs of 260M, trailing behind the likes of WeChat. WB will have to fight off bigger competitors like Tencent and Bytedance, but WB can still succeed by, for instance, carving out a niche of its own. WB, for example, is a popular platform when it comes to trending issues, which includes new product launches. By focusing on trending topics or issues, WB can drive engagement.

Another area that can boost growth is artificial intelligence or AI. AI has taken off in China, especially after the success of DeepSeek. WB has also jumped on the DeepSeek bandwagon and AI in general, which in theory could help WB increase efficiency and thus lower costs, but also open up new areas for growth.

For instance, search has long been dominated by established players with barriers to entry high, but AI gives WB a chance and the means to enter the market by offering functionality that can be used as an alternative. AI-powered search is something that can help WB snatch users away from the traditional search providers. This is an area that has the potential to invigorate growth at WB.

Investor takeaways

WB has been negatively affected by the trade dispute involving tariffs between the U.S. and China, WB’s main market. The stock saw its value slashed and there are no indications that the dispute will be resolved anytime soon. Anyone going long WB needs to be conscious of the possibility WB could be held back by the ongoing dispute surrounding tariffs. Another drop in the stock is not impossible.

With that said, the huge drop in the stock is an opportunity to get in on a stock that is by most measures trading at low valuations to the point WB can be said to be undervalued according to at least one metric. For instance, WB can be had with P/E multiples in the mid-single digits and it is also available at less than book value. The latter suggests a target price of $14 per ADS is achievable since share prices ought to trade closer to book value given the state of the income statement.

Granted, growth has been an issue for WB and it could face further headwinds if tariffs cause growth in China to slow down even more. There is not much WB can do about the uncertainty caused by tariffs, but WB can try to offset the headwinds coming from a slower economy and stiff competition by focusing on AI to lower costs and boost growth. It is still possible for WB to keep the top and the bottom line at least stable, if not better, in the current environment.

WB also pays a generous dividend with a yield in the double digits at 10+%, assuming WB continues to allocate $200M a year to dividend payouts. This is a big plus because it allows shareholders to look past short-term fluctuations in the share price due to the tariff dispute. If yield is important, then WB gets a check mark.

The charts also argue in favor of long WB. The recent selloff in the stock reversed course once it got to a price level where WB was able to find support multiple times before, which suggests support remains in force for WB. The stock is still fairly close to this support level that has held for quite some time, which makes for a good entry point.

I am bullish on WB all things considered. Long WB is more of a tricky proposition if one is looking at WB as a short-term trade and expecting an immediate payoff due to the possibility the stock could be subject to further downward pressure if the tariff dispute escalates, which is a possibility since the dispute remains unresolved. Long WB is best if held for the long term because it will allow shareholders to ride out any short-term volatility that may occur in the immediate future, especially if WB continues to yield in the 10+% range.

Bottom line, the pluses outnumber the minuses for WB. There are risks hanging over WB, including a competitive landscape and an unresolved trade dispute, which longs need to keep a close eye on, but whether it is the dividend, low P/E ratios or the healthy balance sheet, there is enough to warrant being a bull on WB over the long haul.

data.title: Argan: Don't Be Fooled Into Buying This Dip
data.description:
Don't Underestimate The Effects Of The Tariffs Mayhem

What a mayhem! President Trump, the so-called "tariff man" has caused consternation all over the market, as his on-again, off-again policy whiplash has engengered record-setting uncertainties in the market, as the bond markets even showed glimpses of spiraling out of control, although the fears of foreign investors dumping long-term US treasuries are likely overstated. Despite that, Trump’s “Blink Wednesday” also purveys investors an acute reminder about the plausibility of sudden policy u-turns, as the White House executes the president’s Art of the Deal with America’s trading partners, and of course China.

Like many companies involved in the industrials sector, Argan, Inc. (NYSE:AGX) has aptly warned investors about the need to prepare for tariffs and possible retaliations from trading partners as some of the most significant risks entailed in its business operations, as Argan elucidated in its 10K annual report filed at the end of March, a few days ahead of Trump’s Liberation Day on April 3. And we already know that businesses have ramped up their inventory in anticipation of these risks, while consumers have also bolstered their last minute spending, so I believe in the near term, the supply chain effects should be somewhat tempered for AGX.

As I highlighted in my previous Argan update, the company mainly drives its bullish thesis through the anticipated multi-year expansion and surge in electricity demand across AI data centers, manufacturing reshoring opportunities, and also the low-carbon/renewable energy & EV prospects. For the most part of January, I believe the deregulatory and tax cut, and pro-business agenda dominated the business and investing discourse, as the market was primed for a continuation of the optimism that preceded President Trump’s inauguration for his second term at the White House.

However, many business leaders were rightly stunned, as the US government applied shock therapy to compel a comprehensive economic restructuring that could upend "US exceptionalism," while also spurring a global trade slowdown. While we await for more good news from the White House with reference to its concurrent negotiations with trading partners on their respective reciprocal tariffs deals, the China risk could still set us back further as they have moved beyond the pure numbers game (tariff rates) into non-tariff barriers to target the US strategically. Consequently, supply chain resilience for the US economy could be put to the limit, given the convoluted global supply chain that American businesses like Argan are surely exposed to.

AGX has already alerted investors that the impact on [the then proposed] tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China could have an anticipated impact on “the cost of essential construction materials, such as steel and aluminium.” AGX also added that these are “critical components in power plant construction,” suggesting investors shouldn’t downplay the effects of the tariffs and future reprisals on AGX’s operating performance. Already, we have started to experience the impact on America’s tariffs on its neighbors having a marked impact on the aluminium businesses, and we will probably see more of these reports percolating through the economy.

Argan: Strong Backlog. But, Must Still Convert

The silver lining is probably that AGX’s revenue base is primarily in the US, although we still need to assess the impact from the supply chain on its almost $1.4B backlog that it accumulated in FY2025. While it marks a spectacular surge from FY2024, it remains to be seen whether FY2026 might yield another record setting acceleration opportunity, or could we even see a sharp drop off in CapEx spending for the data centers, which is identified as one of the main drivers of growth for AGX and also power demand and electricity generation.

The good news is, natural gas plants are still in demand by AI infrastructure providers, aligned to AGX’s current favorable posture in capitalizing on the industry's resilience to near-term demand shocks, while continuing to build up its long-term backlog for low-carbon or renewable solutions. However, the uncertainties associated with the White House’s aversion and objections to renewable energy projects could stall the forward outlook for this long-term growth vertical for AGX, suggesting we might have to be patient to see this thesis pan out.

Yet, the threat of having two key drivers (data centers and low-carbon projects) encountering noticeable headwinds might not be adequate to mitigate the reshoring optimism that has likely underpinned AGX’s recent recovery from its April 2025 lows. In other words, the qualms into what extent can America’s reshoring drive achieve remains questionable.

Business leaders must be confident that the high tariffs are here to stay and not face a reversal if they decide to reshore to America, even if we consider that inputs, import substitution and supply chain rewiring are possible. Otherwise, these CEOs might not be incentivized to move their well-established manufacturing bases, and consider pausing or delaying their projects until after they gain more clarity.

Moreover, we might also start to see more lawsuits being filed to change President Trump’s constitutional authority to bypass congress in setting these punitive tariffs, as they are starting to gain bipartisan support, as the GOP faces the risks of losing seats in congress in the mid term if the White House somehow manages to send the economy into a recession.

In other words, I believe the risks of AGX’s growth having stalled from this year isn’t immaterial, although the company has already cautioned that repeating a record FY2025 or even its final fiscal quarter seems unrealistic. Yet, the company believes that the growth momentum might recover in the latter half of this year, although I believe the current state of policy ambiguities suggest such optimism might need to be tempered in subsequent quarters, while contemplating a sharp deceleration in growth rates.

Argan: Faces Steep Revenue Growth Deceleration Risks

Revised AGX earnings estimates are aligned with management’s sanguine outlook on a possible pivot in growth rates from the second half of the 2025 calendar year, although questions about whether there’s a need to moderate our exuberance must be contemplated.

With the robust outlook communicated, it’s reasonable to expect a solid trajectory on its multi-year projects roadmap, suggesting AGX’s positivity isn’t assessed as misplaced. However, the threat of facing unforeseen cost increases that could scupper its margins projects, and the increased uncertainties linked to unexpected delays in its revenue conversion might affect growth inflection through the first half of 2026, if a recessionary outlook or a stagflationary scenario play out.

AGX: Valuation Too Pricey

That might have been more palatable, if AGX’s earnings multiples aren’t so aggressively configured, as the near-term cyclicality in its growth verticals might behoove steep multiples compressions if the growth outlook fails to pan out. Such are the high stakes for the company, and it's something that we can no longer afford to ignore under the current administration.

However, AGX’s forward EBITDA multiple of 13.9x remains at the two standard deviations zone over its 10Y average. Sure, bullish investors might argue that Argan is in a new advancement phase underpinned by secular growth drivers, and thus deserving of a higher valuation multiple.

Yet, the need to substantiate its valuations with what seems like pretty aggressive growth rates (in the current uncertain environment) seems too risky to contemplate. Moreover, when I gleaned AGX’s forward PEG ratio of 2.58, it’s almost 70% over its sector median, and analysts have continued to write down sector earnings estimates as I pen this update.

Can Argan really go against the falling tide for that much longer if economic repercussions and headwinds were to batter the US and global economy as the Trump tariffs turmoil rear its ugly head again? Probably, but I don’t think the current multiples give sufficient margin of safety for investors at all (if any).

Buy Or Sell AGX Stock?

When I parsed AGX’s long-term price chart, I believe it’s clear for all to see that the stock had a meteoric rise that arguably started in early 2024, while the market anticipated its massive backlog.

That panned out with the record $1.4B backlog it recorded, although we must always remember that the company has also cautioned that “project backlog amounts may be uncertain indicators of future revenues as project realization may be subject to unexpected adjustments, delays and cancellations.” Hence, we cannot simply assume definitive revenue conversion, and must scrutinize the multitudinous headwinds that could afflict Argan’s supply chain and infra spending in its most important verticals.

Moreover, AGX encountered stiff resistance which I also assessed to be a bull trap that rejected further advances above the $190 level, incidentally the stock’s January 2025 highs. The stock then cratered, falling more than 45% through its March lows, engulfing investors who bought near that January zenoth. However, it seems to have formed a bottom above the $100 level, which also coincides with the 61.8% fibonacci retracement level, auguring well for a potentially sustained bottom.

While I would normally have turned more optimistic after such deep pullbacks to shake out FOMO, I believe the bullish thesis could come under greater threats from the policy vissicitudes of the Trump administration, as we draw closer to the 90-day expiry for his tariff negotiations. Or the non-tariff barriers between the US and China might intensity supply chain risks, possibly hobbling trade flows into a trickle, even if they may not stop completely.

With the bull trap close to the $190 level possibly stemming further upside for now, while navigating what seems like a highly elevated valuation multiple, I urge investors not to buy this dip and recovery, but instead watch from the sidelines to see how it unfolds.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Netflix Saves Capitalism From Itself
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Not Doomed Yet

As everyone knows, the market is doomed and the economy is dead. Liberalized capitalism is finished and we shall all soon be eking out a living in the poor house shoveling biomass into the furnace driving the nearest datacenter. Despite this existential threat, it seems folks are still ponying up for their Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) subscriptions. The company printed a solid quarter in Q1, and guided for another sound level of growth in Q2.

Now this tells us something about the market’s current travails. A leading consumer business is telling us that consumer spending is fine and is expected to continue to be fine. Earlier today, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) told the world that demand for advanced semiconductor devices was strong in Q1 and guided for more strength in Q2. Another breadcrumb telling us that the real economy is holding up better than the narrative-machines would have you believe.

At present, whilst there are some companies and some individuals already adversely affected by the tariff situation, the market selloff has happened in anticipation of more pain to come in the future, not in response to widespread real-economy problems stacking up. As always the market doesn’t know for sure whether more pain will come (and if so, if it will come from tariffs) or whether the Advil will kick in. Investors have sold first and will ask questions later.

Should trade agreements actually fall into place at any level the market deems acceptable, I would expect this market to resolve to the upside because as yet, the real economy is looking fine, so without the worry of what-if, we should see equity indices rise across the board.

Let’s take a look at NFLX numbers to evidence this argument.

Financial Fundamentals

Growth dropped a touch this quarter, to +13% YoY, but this remains very strong. Cash flow margins ticked up from 24% to 25% on a TTM unlevered pretax free cash flow basis. Net leverage ticked up slightly to 0.7x TTM UFCF but this remains very modest debt loading indeed. A very solid quarter.

Guidance? For growth to accelerate. That’s what TSMC said too. Acceleration into Q2. And unlike TSMC, NFLX demand won’t have suffered from any pull-forward effects due to tariffs. If NFLX management believe customer spending is going to see accelerating growth into the next quarter, it suggests the consumer is currently untouched by the rising risks of trade interruptions and costs.

So in the market today we seem to have a selloff caused by multiple compression in anticipation of an adverse hit to earnings arising from the current trade policy travails. Lack of resolution only amplifies these worries of course. And that suggests that if trade relations are settled by agreed tariff rates and other non-tariff matters, the market multiple of earnings can rise.

Valuation Analysis

NFLX today trades at some 11x TTM revenue / 43x TTM unlevered pretax FCF.

That's not cheap for 15% growth in revenues and in cash flows alike, but it isn’t prohibitive and doesn’t suggest to us that the stock should be sold due to excessive valuation.

Stock Chart

We think the stock can climb a little towards a 1.618 Wave 3 extension at $1165 before a selloff, a retest of the 2022 highs, then a final move up. We rate this name at Hold as a result. (Note that unlike most index leaders right now, the stock is holding over both its 50-day and 200-day moving averages).

Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 17 April 2025.

data.title: PLTY For Income Investors, Taming Palantir's Wild Ride
data.description:
I have covered a few YieldMax Income ETFs in my previous writings, and there is no single theme fits all take. The fundamental theory around writing cash secured calls and call spreads, and their pitfalls, is already in Yieldmax’s prospectus and well discussed.

I will evaluate the YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:PLTY) for what it aims to do despite the pitfalls, and that is generating stable sizeable income, riding the volatility of the underlying, while adequately preserving capital. And this I will do a little differently this time. For those who have been following me in my other analyses, will probably know that I usually look at the performance tenure (experience across market conditions), stability of returns and a total return analysis. Fundamentally, I am going to do the same, but in a slightly different way for PLTY, to arrive at a case for investing in it. I will take a set of most frequent scenarios and illustrate outcomes – where possible with data or else conceptually.

Scenario 1 : Underlying Crashes

PLTY was launched in October 2024 and does not have a lot of data or history to feel comfortable about its performance through market cycles – and income plans structured the way YieldMax plays, certainly depend a lot on market cycles when it comes to performance. This is something I have been trying to convey to my readers, who look at historical performance alone.

Feb 18, 2025 to Mar 10, 2025. Palantir (PLTR) crashed by about 38%. How did PLTY do? Did very well actually. Mirrored the fall in terms of the capital NAV. Overall PLTY fell by 40%, only marginally higher, but followed PLTR’s trail without additional losses.

Dividend payouts at the end of Feb 2025, saw a decent uptick, which is expected – almost double of previous distributions. So far PLTY follows the plot.

Scenario 2 : Underlying Shoots Up

Jan 14th 2025 to Feb 18th 2025. In a short span of a month, PLTR shot up 82%. This is a real test for PLTY. I am expecting either payouts to suffer or the underlying NAV capital in PLTY to take the hit as upside is capped. Let us check.

Sure enough, PLTY tracks the trend, but lags overall. At a time when PLTR has shot up 82% in quick time, the PLTY strategy has been able to capture only 55% uptrend. In conjunction with the scenario of sudden downturn discussed in Scenario 1, what this means is the capital went up by 55% and came down by 40% (both scenarios 1 and 2 combined), while the underlying went up by 82% and came down by 38%. Net impact after the two sided move, is that PLTR is now 15% up in all, while PLTY NAV is down by 9%.

The only payout during the upmove period was on Jan 30th 2025 at $2.98 (~2-5% of the NAV of PLTY during the period). And that does not make up for the opportunity loss overall. Taking both the payouts (during scenario 1 and scenario 2) during the uptrend and ensuring correction (the second payout as discussed earlier in scenario 1 being ~10% of PLTY NAV), the overall payouts do not mitigate the opportunity loss in the fund’s capital in a total return sense.

And that is exactly the point I have been trying to get across to my readers in the previous articles as well. Certain market conditions are not great for PLTY type income ETFs (while others are). Your expectation of the market condition is likely a critical factor to consider before investing. For very long term investors, you may end up matching performances at best, with a slightly lesser bumpy ride (in the best of cases). If the strategy underperforms, you will lose out in total return terms (in anything below an optimal performance case for PLTY).

Scenario 3 : Underlying Makes Volatile Moves

The juxtaposition of cases from Jan 15th to Mar 10th 2025 (scenario 1 and scenario 2), discussed as two separate scenarios earlier, replicates just that – a volatile market condition. We have seen the risk of overall underperformance for PLTY in this case. However, combining the payouts to the capital erosion, the actual performance mandate (despite underperformance) will depend on the investor's use case and risk appetite.

Certainly what we saw so far, cannot be called abysmal performance (underperformance in total return sense, yes, but not abysmally) in the conditions gauged. And then there will be other market conditions, where the performance will exceed the underlying’s and on average eventually provide a path of average capital preservation at decent returns, capping downside risks. Sounds good and PLTY follows that thesis thus far.

Scenario 4 : Underlying Goes Down Over Time

No such actual data in the short period of PLTY’s existence. But hypothetically, this is where PLTY should perform very well. Income from written calls should cushion the fall in underlying and return net income to investors in an era when PLTR is declining. PLTY should ace this market – provided the decline is smooth and gradual.

Scenario 5 : Underlying Goes Up Over Time

This is another market which serves PLTY well. In fact, in this case, depending on the structural setup of the options, the fund may gain as much as the underlying and add some income to the investor’s pocket from written calls expiring worthless.

Scenario 6: Underlying is Rangebound

Superimpose Scenario 3 and 4 for this. Again a strong case for an instrument like PLTY. No actual data exists to judge PLTY’s performance in such a situation– I go by what should be in theory.

Is PLTR the Right Underlying

Volatility is what I look for. Not too much such as MSTR, and not too low either. PLTR passes the lower threshold. We have seen the wild price swings in the last quarter alone. Average 252 day IV for PLTR is at 66. On the higher side, but well below MSTR’s 252 day average IV of 105. PLTR definitely qualifies as a good underlying to be able to generate decent income.

Premium Discount and Time of Entry

Strategies like PLTY will be generally need to be timed well for short to medium term investments. On that count, entering now fits the bill perfectly as IV remains elevated, but prices are stabilizing – implying a period of decent capital preservation at high payouts. Of course, it depends on your outlook for the markets going ahead. You need to be first bullish about PLTR and ideally time an entry just after a volatile period, like now. More volatility pending? That’s the call short term investments need to take.

Longer term investors have no such predicament. Over a very long term, markets will evenly dish out different cycles and the plan will go through periods of capital erosion, higher payouts and capital buildup, lower payouts, providing a smoother path to follow PLTR. Only beware – distributions cannot be sustained highs of the current projected 117% pa, understand that.

Another thing I usually check is the premium or discount to NAV, the fund trades at. In the case of PLTY, this is significantly high – deviations of up to 2% are noted. Clearly, that adds another timing dimension to fresh investments – usually I advise spreading the entry over days to minimize this impact, if transaction costs allow.

Final Call

As always, investing in PLTY should come with an indirect belief in PLTR (not in scope of this analysis). If you do and want to ride PLTR with reduced volatility and decent payouts, I would recommend a Buy for PLTY. Timing is right just about now for medium term investors. Long term investors can enter anytime, better now.

Expectations of payout yield should be moderated, but PLTY should most likely provide investors two things – 1) an easier ride on PLTR than owning the stock directly and 2) beat most other kinds of income plans in the longer term (but not at 117% pa return levels).

data.title: IBM Acquiring Hakkoda Enhances Its Transformation From It's Legacy Businesses
data.description:
International Business Machines (NYSE:IBM) has made quite a comeback, as they were written off as a legacy technology company that wasn't adapting to the emerging landscape when Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) exited its position. Over the past 5-years, shares of IBM have made new all-time highs as they spun off their legacy infrastructure businesses to focus on larger ROI opportunities, including cloud, consulting, and artificial intelligence. The strategy has worked, and I remain bullish on IBM, especially after IBM announced that it had acquired Hakkoda to expand its consulting data transformation services. There are still some skeptics regarding how impactful artificial intelligence will be, but the largest companies are committed to allocating hundreds of billions toward CapEx to further their artificial intelligence offerings. IBM has entrenched itself in the technologies of tomorrow, and EPS growth is still projected on the horizon. As the technological landscape changes, I believe that IBM has gotten itself in a position to reap the rewards of where future capital is deployed. I think the future still looks bright, and IBM can be an investment that will thrive in the future.

Following up on my previous article about IBM

I wrote an article about IBM in the middle of January (can be read here), and since then, shares have increased by 10.70% while the S&P 500 has declined by -7.89%. When the dividend is factored in IBMs, the total return is 11.44% over this period. IBM has continued to be a solid investment for investors as it has led to large amounts of capital appreciation with modest dividend income. In my previous article, I discussed how I felt IBM's valuation was still attractive after a strong Q3 report in addition to the opportunities I felt they could capitalize on in the future. I am following up with a new article to discuss how the Hakkoda acquisition can further IBM's transformation and position them for further growth in the future. IBM has proved many bears wrong over the past several years, and I think there is a lot more upside potential and dividend income for long-term investors.

Risks to investing in IBM

While I am still bullish on IBM after making new all-time highs, there are risks to consider before allocating capital to them. Despite IBM being a well-established company focusing on high-growth segments, including cloud computing and artificial intelligence, IBM operates in a competitive industry that is under pressure from the largest companies in the market. IBM's cloud infrastructure represents 2% of the total market as they are significantly behind Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOGL). The large lead from the AMZN, MSFT, and GOOGL could negatively impact IBM's ability to expand its market share in cloud computing. While IBM has pivoted and spun off its legacy business, it still relies on its consulting and infrastructure segments to generate a significant portion of its revenue. If the consulting business slows down or its infrastructure business doesn't innovate quickly compared to its competitors, the market's perception of it could change and take the valuation lower. IBM also has $49 billion in long-term debt, which costs them $1.7 billion in interest expenses. If the Fed takes rates higher, then IBM's margins could compress and impact profitability. Investors should conduct their own due diligence as we could have seen the top in IBM's share price in Q1 2025.

The Hakkoda deal is a strategic acquisition for IBM that will further its entrenchment into artificial intelligence consulting

After acquiring HashiCorp to help build its cloud platform, IBM landed another deal to strengthen its consulting business by acquiring Hakkoda. Hakkoda specializes in data transformation and AI-driven solutions through its consulting business. Hakkoda is an award-winning partner of Snowflake (SNOW), which has established its reputation for migrating, modernizing, and monetizing data throughout the enterprise data management sector. Hakkoda has a portfolio of generative AI-powered assets that accelerate its engagements and strengthen IBM's client experiences. This acquisition aligns with IBM's strategic goals as IBM has heavily invested in artificial intelligence and hybrid cloud solutions since shedding its legacy business. Hakkoda's capabilities in data platform management and artificial intelligence expand and strengthen IBM's consulting business by offering a more comprehensive solution to its clients looking to optimize their data and monetize their digital assets. This acquisition advances IBM's commitment to being a leading consulting firm to help enterprise-scale clients compete in the age of artificial intelligence and gain a competitive advantage over their competitors that aren't making these strategic investments.

I am very bullish on this move by IBM, as Hakkoda's advantage is its ability to migrate, modernize, and help clients monetize their data, with the help of their alignment with SNOW. Hakkoda's expertise in these emerging technologies is critical as enterprises are expanding their appetite to build integrated data platforms that are optimized for speed, cost, and efficiency through leveraging AI-driven operations. Hakkoda's focus on industry-specific solutions, including healthcare & life sciences, and financial services, insurance, supply chain & logistics, retail & CPG, and public sector will not only bring in more customers to IBM's portfolio but expand IBM's ability to service its existing clients in these areas. IBM will be able to offer advanced artificial intelligence solutions and enhance its client's competitive edge. Once Hakkoda's generative AI tools and assets are integrated into IBM's platform, IBM's consulting team will have the ability to deliver results faster and more efficiently for its clients. I believe that the combination of assets will be a key differentiator in the market and make IBM's consulting services more attractive as companies leverage the use of artificial intelligence.

IBM's consulting business is its 2nd largest business segment, as it generated $20.7 billion in revenue during the 2024 fiscal year. While the consulting business only grew by 1% in 2024, it achieved high single-digit growth over a 3-year period, which aligns with IBM's previous projections. IBM had a record-breaking Q4 in signings growth with 23%, which is the highest in its recent history, while producing an 8% YoY increase in its backlog, which indicates strong demand for its consulting services. IBM's GenAI business has grown to over $5 billion since its inception, of which 80% comes from consulting services. I believe that the Hakkoda deal will help expand IBM's consulting revenue, and it could provide its software business with a multiplier effect as the consulting team cross-sells its clients with IBM's software products.

IBM still looks undervalued, and I think there is an opportunity for shares to go higher

When I look through IBM's financials, I see strong margins and increased levels of profitability. Over the past 3-years, IBM has increased its annual revenue by $5.57 billion (9.67%). IBM's gross profit has grown by $4.07 billion (12.91%) over this period to $35.55 billion. This is a gross profit margin of 57.3%, which has increased from 55.66% in 2021. IBM finished 2024 generating $12.4 billion in free cash flow (FCF) which was an increase of 15.49% since 2021. IBM is operating at a 19.98% FCF margin, which is very enticing considering its FCF growth rate. On the bottom line, IBM generated just over $6 billion in net income in 2024, which was a 4.88% increase since 2021. IBM has a 9.71% profit margin and continues to utilize its profitability to make acquisitions and return capital to its shareholders. In 2024, IBM paid $6.15 billion to its investors in dividends while repurchasing $651 million worth of shares.

In addition to becoming a company that has reinvented itself and growing its top and bottom line, IBM has also become a Dividend Aristocrat. IBM has grown its dividends over the past 25 years, and over the past 5 years, the dividends have grown by 1.54%. IBM is paying a dividend of $6.68, which is a 2.8% yield. The payout ratio on IBM's dividend is 64.57%, so there is a lot of room in their EPS to continue future increases. In an environment where the Fed will likely start cutting and work its way to a more neutral, effective rate, IBM's dividend will be growing, and investors can lock in a 2.8% yield at today's levels. When I look back at the first article I wrote about IBM in June 2018 (can be read here), IBM has appreciated by 72.93%, and its total return is 146.71% as more than double the return has been provided through its dividend. IBM was drastically undervalued back then, but these metrics show how powerful a strong dividend program can be in boosting a company's total return.

The analyst community is expecting IBM to continue its EPS expansion over the next several years. IBM is projected to generate $10.77 of EPS in 2025, and it currently trades at 20.88 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2 years, IBM is expected to grow its EPS by 14.3% from $10.77 to $12.31. Investors are still paying 19.38 times 2027 earnings for IBM, which is still a bargain despite IBM trading for above $200 per share. IBM is also expected to grow its revenue on a YoY basis at the mid-single-digit percentage rate, and I think that the latest acquisition of Hakkoda could add additional EPS to the bottom line. IBM is trading at an attractive valuation, especially since enterprises are still in their infancy of deploying their artificial intelligence strategy. The next several years are setting up well for IBM, and I think that we will see several upward revisions in earnings along the way.

Conclusion

IBM's transformation from a legacy tech giant to a leader in cloud computing, consulting, and artificial intelligence showcases its ability to adapt and deliver on its strategic vision. The recent acquisition of Hakkoda has sent a message that IBM will continue building out its enterprise offerings in these areas as it doubles down on the commitment to its strategic vision. This move bolsters IBM's consulting arm with specialized expertise in data transformation and AI-driven solutions, positioning it to meet the rising enterprise demand for innovative technologies. Despite risks such as fierce competition from larger companies and a reliance on its consulting and infrastructure segments, IBM is generating $12.4 billion in free cash flow, which allows it to continue paying a large dividend and make acquisitions. I believe IBM is trading at an attractive valuation with projected EPS growth of 14.3% over the next two years. IBM has produced a compelling blend of capital appreciation and steady income over the past several years, and I believe its recent string of acquisitions will allow IBM to continue this trend. As enterprise clients continue to allocate capital toward their artificial intelligence and cloud infrastructure, IBM is in a strong position to continue its forward growth and generate returns for shareholders.

data.title: CSX Corporation: Treasuries Look Better
data.description:
It’s been about 3 months since I wrote an article titled “CSX: Continue to Avoid. (NASDAQ:CSX). You’ll never guess what my recommendation was. Since then, the shares are down about 16% against a loss of about 13% for the S&P 500, so my very fragile, underdeveloped ego feels somewhat vindicated. I wish this article could be one 1,200 word victory lap, full of self congratulation, but unfortunately you people wouldn’t find that all that interesting, so I’ve got to write about the stock. The company has just announced again, which is what put the name back on my radar. I want to work out whether or not it makes sense to buy at current levels. After all, a stock trading at $27.50 is a much less risky investment than when it’s trading at $33. I’ll make the determination by reviewing the financials, and by comparing the stock to the risk free investment.

As I say repeatedly, and no doubt tiresomely, if a stock isn’t likely to offer superior returns to the risk free alternative, why would you buy it? Stocks come with risk. Treasuries come with risks too, but are far fewer. Therefore the former should offer you superior returns. I hope this point isn’t controversial.

Welcome to my thesis statement. This is the part of the article where I give you the summation of my thoughts pretty early so you have the option to go off and do something more productive with your time than exposing yourself to my self congratulatory screed, questionable humour, and proper spelling. You’re welcome. I’m of the view that CSX’s financial performance was uninspired, and I think this continues a trend that’s been in place for a few years now. Additionally, the dividend would need to grow by an unrealistic amount for stock holders to receive the same cash as Treasury Note holders. If you’re of the view that capital gains may make up for the shortfall to the risk free rate, tell that to people who bought exactly 1 year ago, and are down by about 19%. So, I’m going to continue to recommend avoiding this name until the yield spikes, and/or earnings improve nicely. Thus ends my thesis statement. If you read on from here, that’s on you. I don’t want to read any moaning in the comments section about my awful humour or proper pronunciation of the word “oot.”

Financial Snapshot

In my view, the latest financial results are unimpressive. Compared to the same period last year, revenue and net income are down by 7%, and 26.6% respectively. Operating income is lower by about 22%. If we zoom out a bit, we see that operating income in the quarter just ended is lower by about 14.6% than the first quarter of 2019. I think a reasonable argument could be made to suggest that the railroad has entered a period of slow growth. My evidence for this claim is that revenue in 2024 was lower than it was in 2023, and 2023 was lower than the previous year. So, I don’t expect much in the way of growth for the foreseeable future. This is why the payot ratio has climbed from ~21% to 38.25% today.

Things don’t look much better when we consider the state of the capital structure. Long term debt is higher by about $625 million, or 3.4%, and cash is down by about $336 million, or 23%. Zooming out once again, long term debt is higher today than it was in 2019 by about $3.36 billion, or 21%.

Although the financial performance was unimpressive, I think the dividend is reasonably secure. For that reason, I’d be willing to buy the shares if they’re reasonably priced.

The Stock v Treasury Note

I want to make two points at this juncture, so I’m going to make two points at this juncture. First, in the world of investing, everything’s relative. Second, we’re always looking for the greatest risk-adjusted returns. Please take note of that last sentence my fellow stock investors. We're not seeking "returns." We're seeking "risk-adjusted returns." One of the implications of this point is that a stock, which is generally riskier than the 10 Year Treasury Note, must offer a better return than debt backed by the full faith and credit of Uncle Sam. Put another way, if a stock can’t generate the same level of cash flow as the risk-free rate, why on Earth would you buy it?

In terms of the specifics of comparing a given stock to the Treasury Note, I get at the question “at what rate will the dividend need to grow over the next decade for stock investors to receive the same cash flows as Treasury Note holders?” Please note that I think they should receive more than Treasury holders, given the risks involved, but I’ll burn that bridge when I get to it. So at the moment, the yield on the 10 Year Treasury Note is about 4.4%, and the dividend yield on CSX is about 1.9% at the moment. Per the table below, the dividend would need to grow at a CAGR of about 17.7% for the shareholder to receive the same cash flows as the Treasury Note holder over the next decade. In my view, that’s a very high hurdle, and I think the probability that that growth rate will be achieved is about 0.

A bull will probably argue at this point about the “unassailable moat”, and the fact that dividends aren’t the only source of returns for stockholders. Depending on the stock, capital appreciation also plays a huge part in the returns earned by shareholders. That’s true, both stocks and Treasury Notes hold out the promise of earning a capital gain. I’d say two things in response. First, that argument was just as valid a year ago, and the shares are down a little over 14% since then, so what goes up may also come down. This is where the excess risk in stock investing comes into play. So, you may make more on capital gains, but you “pay” for those returns by taking on more risk, so it’s a wash in my view. Second, Treasury Notes can also generate significant capital gains. I’m of the view that bond yields are, over the long term, a function of growth and inflation expectations, I’m not sanguine about either of those, so I’m of the view that rates are likely heading lower at some point. This drop in rates may offer a decent lift in the price of Treasuries. Being long winded is “on brand” for me, and what I just wrote is a pretty verbose passage to demonstrate that you can earn a capital gain in Treasuries too. The advantage of holding the Treasury is that you make a pretty decent, fully guaranteed cash return while you wait.

Given the above, my feelings haven’t really changed. Treasuries remain relatively attractive, so I’m going to continue to recommend avoiding this name. I’ll become much more interested if the stock starts to trade in the low $20s.
